articles.name: How can I switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding?
articles.title: How can I switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding?
articles.body:
How to remove the forwarding address from the Gmail connector and use standard email forwarding

Tip: Follow the steps below during off-hours to not miss incoming tickets from your customers.

To switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding:

  1. Click the Zendesk Products icon () in the top bar and click Admin Center.
  2. In Admin Center, click Channels in the sidebar.
  3. Under Talk and email, click Email.
  4. Follow this step and the next step if the support address in question is your default address. Hover your mouse to the right of your original Zendesk support address (support@yoursubdomain.zendesk.com).
  5. Click make default if this email address is not already your default support address.
  6. Select disconnect to the right of your external support address, for example, support@company.com to delete the Gmail connector.
  7. Configure email forwarding in Gmail from the external support address to the default support address, for example support@company.com to support@yoursubdomain.zendesk.com.
  8. Select Add address > Connect external address > Email forwarding.
  9. Add the external support address in the Enter current support address field.
  10. Click Go.
  11. If you want this address to become your default support address, hover the mouse to the right of the external support address and click make default.

After you complete the steps above, have an admin of your email domain add the Zendesk entry of include:mail.zendesk.com to the SPF record for the domain of the external support address. For more information, see Allowing Zendesk to send email on behalf of your email domain.

Note: If you use the Gmail Connector in combination with the Send email via Gmail option, Gmail email servers send the notifications and you can locate these notifications in the Sent Items folder in Gmail. After you switch to standard forwarding, Zendesk sends these emails on behalf of your domain and Gmail no longer contains the sent messages.
THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

How do I switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding? How do I remove the forwarding address from the Gmail connector? How do I change to standard email forwarding in my help desk or ticketing system?

Follow these steps to switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding:

  1. Click the Zendesk Products icon in the top bar and click Admin Center.
  2. In Admin Center, click Channels in the sidebar.
  3. Under Talk and email, click Email.
  4. Hover your mouse to the right of your original Zendesk support address or subdomain (support@yoursubdomain.zendesk.com) if the support address in question is your default address.
  5. Click make default if this email address is not already your default support address.
  6. Select disconnect to the right of your external support address (for example, support@company.com) to delete the Gmail connector.
  7. Configure email forwarding in Gmail from the external support address to the default support address (for example, support@company.com to support@yoursubdomain.zendesk.com).
  8. Select Add address Connect external address Email forwarding.
  9. Add the external support address in the Enter current support address field.
  10. Click Go.
  11. Hover the mouse to the right of the external support address and click make default if you want this address to become your default support address.

When is the best time to switch from the Gmail connector to standard email forwarding? How do I avoid missing customer tickets when I change my email setup?

Follow the setup steps during off-hours so you do not miss tickets from your customers or end users.

What must I do after I switch to standard email forwarding? Do I need to update my SPF record when I remove the Gmail connector?

After you complete the steps, have an admin of your email domain add the Zendesk entry of include: mail.zendesk.com to the SPF record for the domain of the external support address.

Will Gmail still save my sent notifications after I change to standard email forwarding? Can I find Zendesk notifications in my Gmail "Sent Items" folder after I disconnect the Gmail connector?

No. After you switch to standard forwarding, Zendesk sends these emails on behalf of your domain or account and Gmail no longer contains the sent messages. If you use the Gmail Connector in combination with the Send email via Gmail option, Gmail email servers send the notifications and you can locate these notifications in the Sent Items folder in Gmail.

articles.name: How to troubleshoot issues with tickets
articles.title: How to troubleshoot issues with tickets
articles.body:
Question

How can I fix general issues with my tickets at Zendesk?

Answer

Tickets keep track of the conversations between your customers and your agents until the issue is solved. This article provides a guide for identifying and resolving the most frequently encountered issues when working with tickets.

Click each section below:

  • Why is my ticket marked as Open?
  • Why did the ticket status change?
  • Why does replying to a ticket create a new one?
  • Who modified my ticket?
  • Can I customize a ticket status?

Why is my ticket marked as Open?

The New status indicates that no action was taken on a ticket. Once the status of a ticket changes from New, it can never be set back to New.

The status of a ticket can change to Open if:

  • An agent manually changed the status of the ticket to Open.
  • A ticket was assigned to an agent. When a new ticket is assigned to an agent, the ticket status automatically changes to Open.
  • If your account only has one agent, all tickets are automatically assigned to that agent, and the status of the ticket changes to Open.
  • When a ticket is assigned to a group with one member, that agent becomes the ticket's assignee, and the status of the ticket changes to Open.
  • When an end user replies to a Pending, Solved, or On-hold ticket, the ticket status changes to Open.
  • An automation or a trigger can change the status of the ticket to Open. To find out if a business rule acts on the group of the ticket, see the article: Viewing all events of a ticket.

For more information, see the article: About the system ticket rules.

Why did the ticket status change?

The status of a ticket can change for multiple reasons. Besides the reasons described in the previous section, there are other possible causes for a change in ticket status.

  • An agent manually changed the status of the ticket.
  • A business rule changed the status of the ticket. To find out if a business rule acts on the group of the ticket, see the article: Viewing all events of a ticket.
  • Tickets close automatically 28 days after they're set to solved, regardless of any triggers or automations.

For more information, see the article: About the system ticket rules.

Why does replying to a ticket create a new one?

When Zendesk receives a new email, it checks the message to determine if the email should be added as an update to an existing ticket, or if it should create a new one. This is what happens:

  1. A user sends a message to Support.
  2. Zendesk searches for the message-ID of the email.
    • If the message-ID matches the message-ID associated with an existing ticket, the email threads to that parent ticket.
    • If the message-ID doesn't match any message-ID associated with an existing ticket, the email creates a new ticket.

View the original email to confirm if the email Message-IDs match up. For more information, see the article: Why do emails thread to the wrong ticket?

Additionally, if the user replies to a ticket with a Closed status, their reply creates a follow-up ticket. For other situations in which replies to a ticket create new tickets, see the article: Why do replies to email-target notifications create a new ticket? 

Who modified my ticket?

See all the updates and notifications that happened on a ticket if you check the ticket events. Whenever you are unsure why a property of your ticket changed, check the events of that same ticket as explained in the video below.

The events of the ticket can also be used to troubleshoot business rules. For more information, see the article: How to troubleshoot your triggers.

Can I customize a ticket status?

If you use the Zendesk Agent Workspace, create a new custom ticket status that corresponds to one of the ticket status categories (New, Open, Pending, On-hold, Solved). For more information, see the articles:

  • Can I add custom ticket statuses?
  • Creating custom ticket statuses

The priority ticket field and its values cannot be edited or modified. For a workaround, follow the article: Can I edit the priority field?

For more information, see this article: Why can't I access this ticket?

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

What is a ticket? Why do I use tickets? What do tickets track?

Tickets keep track of the conversations between your customers or end users and your agents or support persons until your team solves the issue.

Can I set a ticket status back to "New"? Can you change a ticket progress back to "New"? Can an agent reset a state to "New"?

No. Once the status or progress of a ticket changes from New, you can never set it back to New. The New status indicates that your team took no action on a ticket.

Why is my ticket marked as "Open"? Why did my ticket progress change to "Open"? What causes a ticket state to change to "Open"?

A ticket status or progress changes to Open for several reasons. An agent or support person manually changes the status to Open, or the system assigns a ticket to an agent. When the system assigns a new ticket to an agent, the ticket status automatically changes to Open. If your account contains only one agent, the system automatically assigns all tickets to that agent and changes the status to Open. When a team assigns a ticket to a group or department with one member, that agent becomes the ticket assignee, and the status changes to Open. Additionally, the status changes to Open when an end user or customer replies to a Pending, Solved, or On-hold ticket, or when an automation or time-based rule or a trigger or auto-reply changes the status.

When do tickets close automatically? What is the hard limit for a "Solved" ticket to close? How long does a ticket stay "Solved" before it closes?

Tickets close automatically 28 days after your team sets them to Solved. This hard limit applies regardless of any triggers or auto-replies, or automations or time-based rules.

Why does a reply to a ticket create a new one? Why do replies create new tickets? How does the ticketing system route incoming emails?

When the help desk or ticketing system receives a new email, it checks the message to determine if the email adds as an update to an existing ticket, or if it creates a new one. Follow these steps to see how the system handles messages:

  1. A user or customer sends a message to Support.
  2. The system searches for the message-ID of the email.

If the message-ID matches the message-ID associated with an existing ticket, the email threads to that parent ticket. If the message-ID does not match any message-ID associated with an existing ticket, the email creates a new ticket. Additionally, if the user replies to a ticket with a Closed status, their reply creates a follow-up ticket.

Who modified my ticket? How do I see who updated my ticket? Where can I view ticket updates and notifications?

You see all the updates and notifications that happened on a ticket when you check the ticket events. Whenever you are unsure why a property of your ticket changed, check the events of that same ticket. You can also use the events of the ticket to troubleshoot business rules.

Can I customize a ticket status? Can I add a custom ticket progress? Can you create unique states in the inbox or dashboard?

Yes. If you use the Agent Workspace or inbox, you can create a new custom ticket status. This custom status must correspond to one of the ticket status categories: New, Open, Pending, On-hold, or Solved.

Can I edit the priority field? Can I modify priority values? What are the limitations of the priority ticket field?

No. You cannot edit or modify the priority ticket field and its values.

articles.name: Error code 550 due to Reject Direct Send feature in Microsoft Exchange
articles.title: Error code 550 due to Reject Direct Send feature in Microsoft Exchange
articles.body:
Disclaimer: This article is provided for instructional purposes only. To get more information in regards to these scenarios, we recommend to get in touch with the support team at Microsoft. 

Issue symptoms

  • Users within your company's email domain do not receive outgoing emails from your Zendesk account.
  • Email delivery failure notifications to those recipients include an error of Recipient server rejected email. Status code: 550
  • Delivery logs show a more specific error of 550 5.7.68 TenantInboundAttribution; Direct Send not allowed for this organization from unauthorized sources.
  • Your organization has the Reject Direct Send feature enabled in Microsoft Exchange.

These errors occur because your organization's Exchange policies are actively blocking emails from reaching their intended recipients. This happens when Zendesk isn’t recognized as an authorized sender for your external support address.

Resolution steps

These errors originate from Microsoft, not Zendesk. Your email administrator is responsible for reviewing the error message and verifying your email configuration. However, Zendesk offers basic guidance to help you set up your external email address successfully.

To resolve outgoing email delivery issues, ensure your external support address is properly configured and meets all setup requirements:

  • Allowing Zendesk to send email on behalf of your email domain

If you continue to experience the issues described in this article, consider one of these solutions:

  • Directly connect your non-Zendesk Microsoft Exchange-based email servers to your Zendesk Support instance using the Exchange Connector. This allows you to keep the Reject Direct Send setting enabled because with this setup, all email notifications are sent by your own Exchange server. Please note the limitations documented in the article on the Exchange Connector.

  • Instead of enabling the Reject Direct Setting, use a Transport rule to block, redirect or quarantine undesired emails, and exempt emails from Zendesk from the Rule, as described in the Microsoft FAQs. TheX-Mailer: Zendesk Mailer header may be used to identify mail sent from Zendesk. Your email administrator is responsible for completing this setup, and Zendesk Support cannot provide guidance on its specific configuration.

    THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

    Why do users within my company or customer account domain not receive emails from Zendesk Support? Why do email delivery notifications show error "Recipient server rejected email. Status code: 550"? What causes the delivery log error "550 5.7.68 Tenant InboundAttribution; Direct Send not allowed for this organization from unauthorized sources"?

    Your company or customer account policies block emails to recipients. This issue occurs when you enable the **Reject Direct Send** feature in Microsoft Exchange and Microsoft Exchange does not recognize Zendesk as an authorized sender for your external support address.

    How do I fix the "Status code: 550" email delivery failure? How do I resolve the "Reject Direct Send" error in Microsoft Exchange? How can I ensure users receive emails from my ticketing system or help desk?

    Your email administrator must review the error message and verify your email configuration. You can resolve email delivery issues when you ensure your external support address meets setup requirements. Choose one of these options to resolve the issue:

    1. Configure your external support address to allow Zendesk to send email on behalf of your email domain.
    2. Connect your non-Zendesk Microsoft Exchange-based email servers to your Zendesk Support or help desk instance with the **Exchange Connector**.
    3. Use a Transport rule to block, redirect, or quarantine emails, and exempt emails from Zendesk from the rule.

    Can I keep the "Reject Direct Send" setting enabled in Microsoft Exchange? Can I use my own Exchange server to send Zendesk notifications?

    Yes. You can keep the **Reject Direct Send** setting enabled if you connect your non-Zendesk Microsoft Exchange-based email servers to your Zendesk Support or ticketing system instance. The **Exchange Connector** allows this setup because your own Exchange server sends all email notifications.

    How do I identify email notifications sent from Zendesk? How can my email administrator identify mail from Zendesk Support?

    Your email administrator can use the **X-Mailer: Zendesk Mailer** header to identify mail from Zendesk. The administrator can use this header to exempt Zendesk mail from a Transport rule.

articles.name: Error code 550 5.7.x in Microsoft Exchange
articles.title: Error code 550 5.7.x in Microsoft Exchange
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

  • End users receive a bounce back notification from Microsoft after they send an email to a support address.
  • The notification starts with: Your message could not be delivered.
  • The message includes an error or status code with 550 5.7.x.
  • The emails don't reach Zendesk.

The text of the message will be different for each underlying issue. However, the main problem remains the same: the user's email was rejected before it could reach Zendesk.

Resolution steps

These errors are generated by Microsoft, not Zendesk. Zendesk cannot assist in resolving these errors. If your users suddenly report bounce back notifications and error messages, contact your email administrator. They will need to review the error message and your email configuration.

Note: This type of error can occur on a support address that previously worked. That typically means something has changed. For example, if Microsoft implements a new policy for email forwarding, previous addresses may not meet the new requirements without an update.

The email administrator for your email domain can investigate and resolve these errors. Microsoft may have updated its policies around automatic email forwarding, and it may affect support addresses that synced in the past. For more information, see this article: Forwarding incoming email from your existing email address to Zendesk Support.

Alternatively, you can connect your non-Zendesk email servers, based on Microsoft Exchange, to your Zendesk account with the latest Microsoft Exchange connector. For more information, see this article: Connecting your Microsoft Exchange account to Zendesk.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

What is the "550 5.7.x" error code? Why do end users or customers receive a "Your message could not be delivered" bounce back notification? Why do emails fail to reach Zendesk Support or your help desk?

This error indicates that Microsoft rejected the email from the requester or end user before it could reach Zendesk Support or your ticketing system. Microsoft generates these errors, which typically means something changed in your email configuration. This issue can occur on a support address that worked in the past if Microsoft updates its policies to forward email automatically.

Can Zendesk fix the "550 5.7.x" error code? Does Zendesk Support or your help desk assist with these Microsoft Exchange errors?

No. Microsoft generates these errors, not Zendesk. Zendesk cannot assist you to resolve these errors. The email administrator or agent for your email domain must investigate and resolve the issue.

How do you fix the "550 5.7.x" error code? What steps resolve the "Your message could not be delivered" error for a support address?

You can resolve these errors if you perform one of the following actions:

  1. Contact your email administrator to review the error message and your email configuration.
  2. Connect your non-Zendesk email servers, based on Microsoft Exchange, to your Zendesk account with the latest Microsoft Exchange connector.

articles.name: I am not receiving notifications of new tickets
articles.title: I am not receiving notifications of new tickets
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

I don't receive an email when new tickets are created.

Resolution steps

If you don't receive these notifications, check these possible causes:

Deactivated trigger

Often, notifications stop because a trigger that sent the notification is deactivated.

Verify if a trigger sends a notification when a ticket is created. To check this: check the ticket events for any trigger that notifies the team. The default trigger that sends these notifications is Notify all agents of received request.

If you don't see a trigger that notifies you, view the deactivated triggers and check if any require activation. You can also build a new trigger that mirrors the functionality of one of the default triggers if they're deleted.

Blocked email

If a trigger runs and sends a notification but you still don't receive the email, the issue is likely outside of Zendesk. Check the spam or junk email folder. An admin can check if rules block emails from certain addresses or domains. For further assistance, see Troubleshooting email deliverability.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

Do deactivated triggers or auto-replies stop new ticket alerts? Can a deactivated notification rule stop email alerts for my team or support persons when new tickets are created? Does the default trigger "Notify all agents of received request" fail if it is deactivated?

Yes. Notifications often stop because a trigger or auto-reply that sent the notification is deactivated. Verify if a trigger or notification rule sends a notification when a ticket is created. To check this, use these action steps:

  1. Check the ticket events for any trigger or auto-reply that notifies the team or support person.
  2. View the deactivated triggers if you do not see a trigger that notifies you.
  3. Check if any require activation.

The default trigger that sends these notifications is Notify all agents of received request. If a trigger runs and sends a notification but you still do not receive the email, the issue is likely outside of Zendesk or your ticketing system. Check the spam or junk email folder, or ask an admin to check if rules block emails from certain addresses or domains.

Can I recreate a deleted default trigger or auto-reply? How do I restore the default notification rule if it is deleted? What can I do if a default trigger is deleted from my ticketing system?

You can build a new trigger or auto-reply that mirrors the functionality of one of the default triggers if they are deleted.

articles.name: Delay in ticket creation from email
articles.title: Delay in ticket creation from email
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

  • Your email server is sending emails successfully to your Zendesk support address
  • Some of the emails sent to your Zendesk support address are not creating tickets or create tickets after a long time delay
  • The ticket creation timestamp does not match the timestamp from the email which created the ticket

Resolution steps

There are several potential causes for delay or failed ticket creation from email. Check below for the possible causes and how to resolve them.

Emails are in your suspended tickets queue

Check your suspended tickets queue for the missing emails. There are several causes of ticket suspension which can impact the ability for an email to create a ticket, such as the use of email for bulk ticket creation instead of the API and channels framework.

When you recover an email from the suspended tickets queue, the displayed creation date can differ depending on the ticket view.

  • In the conversations view, the creation date reflects when the email was originally received.
  • In the events view, the creation date reflects when you recover the suspended email and convert it into a ticket.
  • In the ticket audit, the creation date reflects when you recover the suspended email and convert it into a ticket.

An active service incident is impacting email delivery or ticket creation

On rare occasions a service incident can prevent the creation of tickets through email. Check for an active service incident on Zendesk's status page for emails not creating tickets.

You use Gmail and your connection from Google to Zendesk was interrupted

If you use the Gmail connector and do not see emails creating tickets, check the status of your support address. Sometimes a disconnection can occur if you update your Gmail password.

To resolve this issue, reconnect Gmail to your support addresses and resend any missing emails as needed.

For more information, see Troubleshooting email deliverability.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

What is the suspended tickets queue? Why do emails go to the suspended tickets queue? Why do bulk emails fail to create tickets?

The Suspended tickets queue holds emails when ticket creation fails. This suspension occurs when you send bulk emails to create tickets instead of using the API to connect to other apps or channels framework.

Does the ticket creation date change when you recover a suspended email? How does the creation timestamp behave in different ticket views? Does the ticket audit show when you recover an email?

Yes, the displayed creation date varies by your ticket view when you recover an email from the Suspended tickets queue. In the Conversations view, the creation date reflects when you received the email. In the Events view, the creation date reflects when you recover the suspended email and convert it into a ticket. In the Ticket audit, the creation date reflects when you recover the suspended email and convert it into a ticket.

Can a Gmail password update stop email ticket creation? Why does the connection from Google to Zendesk disconnect? How do you fix a disconnected Gmail support address?

Yes, an update to your Gmail password disconnects the Gmail connector and prevents ticket creation. Check the status of your Support address if emails do not create tickets in your ticketing system or help desk. To resolve this issue, complete these steps:

  1. Reconnect Gmail to your Support addresses.
  2. Resend any missing emails as you need.

See Troubleshooting email deliverability for more information.

Can a service incident prevent ticket creation from email? Why do emails fail to create tickets during an outage? Where do you check for Zendesk service disruptions?

Yes, on rare occasions an active service incident prevents email ticket creation. Check Zendesk's status page for an active service incident if emails do not create tickets in your ticketing system or help desk.

articles.name: How do I change my Zendesk subdomain if my company is renamed?
articles.title: How do I change my Zendesk subdomain if my company is renamed?
articles.body:
Question

My company changed names and I want to update my Zendesk subdomain. What are the steps to change my subdomain?

Answer

Account owners can change the subdomain of the account. For instructions on how to do it, see Renaming your subdomain.

Note: Renaming your subdomain may cause unintended side effects. Make sure you understand the considerations before you make a change.

If you decide to rename your subdomain, Zendesk recommends you configure host mapping in your account, educate your customers about the new URL, and then change your subdomain. Host mapping allows you to change the external-facing URL of the help center with the least impact on your customers.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

Can I change my Zendesk subdomain or brand if my company or organization is renamed? Can an account owner rename the subdomain or account instance?

Yes. Account owners can change the subdomain of the account.

Who has permissions to rename the Zendesk subdomain or instance? What role or access level do I need to change my brand account URL?

Account owners possess the required permissions to change the subdomain of the account.

What is the best practice before I change my subdomain or brand instance? Why should I configure host mapping or a custom URL before renaming?

Zendesk recommends you configure host mapping in your account, educate your customers about the new URL, and then change your subdomain. Host mapping allows you to change the external-facing URL of the help center or knowledge base with the least impact on your customers.

Does renaming my subdomain cause unintended side effects or technical issues? Are there risks or limitations when I rename my brand instance?

Renaming your subdomain may cause unintended side effects. Make sure you understand the considerations before you make a change.

articles.name: What are the most common Voice error messages?
articles.title: What are the most common Voice error messages?
articles.body:
Question

What are the most common Zendesk Voice (formerly Talk) error messages?

Answer

See common error messages and resolution steps in Voice below.

Call failed because this number is no longer verified

The error message Call failed because this number is no longer verified appears when Twilio reclaims a non-compliant number due to regulatory reasons. This means the number is no longer in the account and you cannot use it for outbound calls. Contact Zendesk Customer Support for further help.

Invalid number

The Invalid number error usually means the number you enter is incorrect. However, it can also appear if you try to call a premium number, which is a number with a cost to call. Twilio does not support these numbers.

Outbound Calling disabled

The Outbound Calling disabled error message appears if you do not have outbound calls activated. You must have at least one number activated for outbound calls.

To enable outbound calls:

  1. In Admin Center, click Channels in the sidebar, then select Talk and email > Talk
  2. Select Lines, and open the line that shows the error message
  3. Under Settings, activate Allow outbound calls?

Your browser has suspended your audio. Click here to re-enable

To fix this issue, see Why does the voice icon display a red exclamation mark?. By default, Chrome and Edge browsers suspend audio auto-play when you open new pages or tabs. When the browser suspends the audio, a small red icon over the voice icon appears. The agent can click anywhere on the page and the browser restores the audio.

You cannot make calls to this country

Contact Zendesk Customer Support and provide the number you try to call.

Call blocked

The Call blocked error message shows up when Twilio identifies a number as a high risk of fraud, or due to regulatory reasons. Contact Zendesk Customer Support for further help.

Cannot connect to number

The number you call is not formatted correctly. The international standard is E.164 and can have a maximum of 15 digits.

[+] [country code] [subscriber number including area code]

If the number is valid, see Talk error: Cannot connect to number.

No answer

This message may not be visible on some occasions. This is known behavior.

Zendesk shows the No answer message if the receiving party does not accept the call. This happens if the number you call is busy, or not turned on, and that number does not have a voicemail.

Call the number again later. If this message continues to display, use a tool such as numverify to confirm whether the number you dial is valid.

Sorry, Talk is unavailable right now

Agents see this error if they disconnect from the internet or if they have network issues. The message may also show up if Zendesk or Twilio has a service incident.

There was an error completing your call

This error appears when Talk is not able to place an outgoing call. This happens if Zendesk or Twilio has an outage.

Talk is experiencing an issue […]

Zendesk shows this error message when there are connection delays between your computer and account. Calls route to the queue, but issues exist when routing the call to agents. A delay can also happen when opening the new ticket for the call you answer.

When Zendesk recovers and calls flow normally again, hard refresh your browser to clear this error.

If you see this message frequently, contact Zendesk Customer Support with timestamps and the timezone of the event.

Zendesk Talk encountered an error. Try enabling again. (EC5)

EC errors often occur due to your network or router configuration. This can happen anywhere along the network, but often results from local network limitations or from a computer that does not meet the minimum requirements to make the connection.

For troubleshooting steps for this error, see Troubleshooting: Issues with Zendesk Voice (formerly Talk).

Verification could not be submitted for review. Give it a moment and try again

When you upload a business verification document to purchase a Voice phone number, ensure the requirements have a green tick before proceeding. For more information, see Verifying your business for purchasing a Zendesk phone number.

International calling is disabled by default. Contact our Customer Support team if you want to enable international calling

To enable international calling on your account, the account owner must contact Zendesk Customer Support. Added fees apply to the cost of international calls.

There's an audio problem

If the call audio quality drops, you'll see a warning icon in the Voice Professional or Enterprise console.

If you see this warning, which is a red speaker with an x, check that your headset is plugged in and the microphone input levels are high enough. Also, ensure you have not muted the microphone. Finally, try a different headset to ensure the one you use is not faulty.

For more information about getting the best audio quality, see Preparing to offer voice support.

articles.name: I can't reassign a ticket
articles.title: I can't reassign a ticket
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

I can't reassign a ticket. The ticket is stuck with one of our agents or groups. When an agent updates the Assignee field, the ticket automatically reassigns to the same agent or agent group. I can't update the group or assignee on the ticket.

Resolution steps

In many instances, this issue is a result of a trigger that meets the condition of the ticket. The trigger makes the change automatically upon submitting the ticket to save the changes.

To pinpoint the trigger likely causing the reassignment, view the events of a ticket and check the triggers that fired on the ticket during the latest update. Change the properties of the ticket to not meet the conditions of the trigger or update the conditions of the trigger.

For more information to help you solve this issue, see this article: Troubleshooting triggers and tickets.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

Why can I not reassign a ticket? Why does a ticket automatically reassign to the same agent or team? Why is a ticket stuck with one of our agents or groups?

This issue occurs when a trigger or notification rule meets the conditions of the ticket. The trigger makes the change automatically upon submitting the ticket to save the changes.

How do I fix a ticket that will not change its assignee or group? How do I pinpoint the trigger or notification rule causing ticket reassignment?

  1. View the events of a ticket.
  2. Check the triggers or notification rules that fired on the ticket during the latest update.
  3. Change the properties of the ticket to not meet the conditions of the trigger or notification rule, or update the conditions of the trigger or notification rule.

articles.name: Why do my SMS messages fail to send?
articles.title: Why do my SMS messages fail to send?
articles.body:
Question

Scenario A

I receive one of the error messages below when I send an outbound Text message:

  • We couldn't deliver this message
  • We couldn't send the message
Scenario B

When I review the message details in my Text history, the status of my message is either Undelivered or Failed:

Why are my SMS messages failing?

Answer

Multiple reasons exist for SMS message failure. Common reasons why SMS messages fail to send:

  • The destination handset is unreachable
  • The destination phone number doesn't support Text
  • The end user sent an opt-out code to one of my messages. For more information on opt-outs, see About opt-out keywords for Zendesk Text.
  • The destination phone number is located in a country where the Text number doesn't support SMS long code international messaging. For example, Zendesk US text numbers can't send SMS messages to UK numbers. For more information, see Twilio's SMS guidelines.

If SMS messages still fail to send or if I need my US/CA toll-free numbers verified, Contact Zendesk Customer Support for further help. with the ticket ID where the SMS message failed.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

Why do my SMS messages fail to send? Why does my text message status say Undelivered or Failed? What causes Zendesk Text outbound messages to fail?

SMS messages fail to send because the destination handset is unreachable, or the destination phone number does not support Text or text messaging. Outbound messages also fail if the end user or customer sends an opt-out code to one of your messages, or if the destination phone number is in a country where your Text number does not support SMS long code international messaging.

Why do I see "We couldn't deliver this message" when sending text messages? Why do I get the error "We couldn't send the message" in Zendesk Text?

You receive these errors due to several common issues. The destination handset is unreachable, the destination phone number does not support text messaging, or the end user or customer sent an opt-out keyword. Additionally, these errors happen when your Text number does not support SMS long code international messaging for the destination country, such as when US text numbers attempt to send SMS messages to UK numbers.

How do I fix failed or undelivered SMS messages in Zendesk? What should I do if my US or CA toll-free numbers need verification?

If your SMS messages still fail to send or you need your US/CA toll-free numbers verified, contact Zendesk Customer Support or our team for further help. Ensure you provide the ticket ID where the SMS message failed when you reach out to support.

articles.name: I can't add new agents and admins
articles.title: I can't add new agents and admins
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

I can't add new agents and admins or see this error: Subscription limit reached.

Resolution steps

You might not be able to add team members if you don't have permission or if you do not have seats available.

To resolve this issue, follow these steps:

Step 1: Verify your own role

Use this step if you do not see the Create team member button.

  1. In Admin Center, click People in the sidebar, then select Team > Team members
  2. Verify that Create team member appears in the top right corner
  3. Check the Support role next to your Name
  4. If you don't see Admin next to your name, you may not be able to add new users. Ask an account owner or an admin to change your role

Step 2: Ensure that you have agent seats available

  • In Admin Center, click People in the sidebar, then select Team > Team members
  • Verify the balance under Seats remaining

  • If you see 0 and you get an error when you try to add a new agent or admin, you don't have seats available

    Note: If the count seems incorrect, or light agents are available but you cannot add more, verify your input and list against this article: What counts as an agent license in Zendesk?

Step 3: Free up seats

Use one of these options to free up seats:

  • Unselect sub-roles and products for potential light agents or contributors
  • Downgrade agents to end users

  • Ask your account owner to purchase additional licenses for new users

    THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

    Why can I not add new agents and admins? Why is the Create team member button missing? What does the "Subscription limit reached" error mean?

    You cannot add team members or staff members if you lack permission, or if you do not have seats or licenses available in your subscription.

    How do I verify my role or permissions to add team members? How can I check if I am an admin?

    1. In Admin Center, click People in the sidebar, then select Team Team members.
    2. Verify that Create team member appears in the top right corner.
    3. Check the Support role next to your Name.
    4. Ask an account owner or an admin to change your role if you do not see Admin next to your name.

    How do I check if I have agent seats or licenses available? How do I verify the balance under Seats remaining?

    1. In Admin Center, click People in the sidebar, then select Team Team members.
    2. Verify the balance under Seats remaining.

    How do I free up seats or agent licenses? How do I remove users to add new agents?

    You can use one of these options to free up seats:

    1. Unselect sub-roles and products for potential light agents, read-only agents, or contributors.
    2. Downgrade agents or support persons to end users or customers.
    3. Ask your account owner to purchase additional licenses or subscriptions for new users.

articles.name: Why are CCed users not receiving emails?
articles.title: Why are CCed users not receiving emails?
articles.body:
Question

When I CC a user, they are not receiving an email. How do I make sure the people I CC get a notification?

Answer

Email addresses that are CC'ed on a ticket rely on triggers to send the message. If CC'ed people are not getting notified, there is an issue with the trigger.

The standard trigger named Notify requester and CCs of comment update is commonly used to send notifications to CC'ed users. However, your team may have changed that trigger.

  1. Check your ticket events to identify the exact trigger that fired

  2. Edit that trigger to make sure its actions include Other > Notify by > User email | Object > Ticket > (requester and CCs)

  3. If your trigger setup is correct and you still encounter an issue with email delivery, verify your email configuration and support address setup. In accounts with Agent Workspace turned on, you can view reasons for email delivery failure from the ticket to help you identify the cause of the issue.

    THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

    Why are CCed users not receiving emails? Why are copied customers not getting notifications? Why do auto-replies fail for CCs? How do I make sure the people I CC get a notification?

    Email addresses that you CC or copy on a ticket rely on triggers or notification rules to send the message. If CCed users or end users do not get notifications, an issue exists with the trigger. The standard trigger named Notify requester and CCs of comment update commonly sends notifications to CCed users, but your team or support person may change that trigger.

    How do I fix a trigger for CCed user notifications? How do I edit notification rules for copied users? How do I configure ticket events to notify CCs?

    You can resolve email delivery issues for CCs by updating your automated actions. Follow these steps:

    1. Check your ticket events to identify the exact trigger that fired.
    2. Edit that trigger to make sure its actions include Other > Notify by > User email | Object > Ticket > (requester and CCs).
    3. Verify your email configuration and support address setup if the trigger setup is correct and you still encounter an issue with email delivery.

    How do I view email delivery failures in the inbox? Where do I see why emails fail in Agent Workspace or dashboard? How do I find the cause of email delivery issues on a ticket?

    In accounts with Agent Workspace or inbox turned on, you can view reasons for email delivery failure from the ticket to help you identify the cause of the issue.

articles.name: Troubleshooting triggers and tickets
articles.title: Troubleshooting triggers and tickets
articles.body:
If your triggers do not appear on tickets or create errors in Zendesk, use this guide to troubleshoot any issues.

Tip: If you have the Copilot add-on, review the AI recommendations to manage and optimize your business rules. Copilot flags redundant and unused business rules, and provides trigger suggestions based on intelligent triage predictions.

This article contains the topics below:

  • Verify the ticket events
  • Check your trigger conditions
  • Verify the trigger order
  • Triggers send emails but customers do not receive emails
  • Test your trigger after changes

Debug your trigger setup by following these steps.

Disclaimer: Do not amend or deactivate standard triggers with Notify requester in the title. Email communication relies on these triggers. Whenever agents add public comments to tickets, in the back-end, those triggers send emails to your customers.

Verify the ticket events

If you're not sure if a trigger ran on a ticket, use the steps below.

  1. Open your ticket events, by adding /events at the end of the URL of your ticket
  2. Search the word trigger to determine which trigger caused automated actions
  3. If you do not see the name of your trigger, verify the conditions of that same trigger

Check your trigger conditions

If you expected a trigger to appear on the ticket, but it doesn't appear, open your trigger and compare each condition with the ticket of your choice.

Common errors include tickets that do not meet all your conditions at once, or your conditions are not mathematically possible. Correct the conditions of your triggers and match your tickets, for example:

  • If your conditions include a specific organization or channel, but your ticket does not meet those conditions, the trigger will not run
    • Instead, ensure the conditions of your trigger match your tickets
  • If multiple ticket statuses appear below Meet ALL of the following conditions, your trigger will never run, because a ticket can only have one status at a time
    • Instead, add these multiple Ticket > Status conditions below Meet ANY of the following conditions

Verify the trigger order

If your trigger appears at the wrong time in tickets, or relies on another trigger to work, verify the order of your list of triggers. Where your trigger appears in the list affects the order of execution.

Triggers send emails but customers do not receive emails

If the trigger and email appear on the ticket events, but your end users do not receive these emails, follow the email troubleshooting guide and ensure that there wasn't a CC issue.

Test your trigger after changes

Once you corrected conditions or the order of your triggers, create a test ticket and ensure you are satisfied with the result.

THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

What is the Copilot or AI Assistant add-on? Why should I use the Agent assist features? How does Copilot optimize my auto-reply rules?

The Copilot or AI Assistant or Agent assist add-on helps you manage and optimize your business rules. It flags redundant and unused business rules and provides trigger or auto-reply suggestions based on intelligent triage predictions.

Can I change standard notification rules? Should I deactivate "Notify requester" auto-replies? Can I edit standard triggers?

Do not amend or deactivate standard triggers or notification rules with "Notify requester" in the title. Email communication relies on these rules. These triggers send emails to your customers or end users when agents add public comments to tickets.

How do I know if an auto-reply ran on a ticket? How do I check ticket events for a notification rule? Where do I see which trigger caused automated actions?

If you are not sure if a trigger or auto-reply ran on a ticket, use these steps to verify the ticket events:

  1. Open your ticket events by adding /events at the end of the URL of your ticket.
  2. Search the word trigger to determine which trigger caused automated actions.
  3. Verify the conditions of that same trigger if you do not see the name of your trigger.

Why does my auto-reply never run? Can a trigger have multiple ticket statuses under "Meet ALL"? Why does my notification rule fail for multiple ticket states?

A trigger or auto-reply never runs if multiple ticket statuses or states appear below Meet ALL of the following conditions because a ticket can only hold one status or progress level at a time. Instead, add these multiple Ticket Status conditions below Meet ANY of the following conditions.

Why is my notification rule not appearing on a ticket? What causes trigger condition errors? Why does my auto-reply fail to match a company account?

Triggers or notification rules do not run if a ticket does not meet all your conditions at once or if your conditions are not mathematically possible. Ensure the conditions of your trigger match your tickets. For example, if your conditions include a specific channel or a specific Organization or Company or Customer Account but your ticket does not meet those conditions, the trigger will not run.

Does the order of the trigger list matter? Why does my auto-reply execute at the wrong time? How does the list position affect notification rules?

Where your trigger or auto-reply appears in the list affects the order of execution. Verify the order of your list of triggers if your trigger appears at the wrong time in tickets or relies on another trigger to work.

Why do users not receive emails from my triggers? What do I do if an auto-reply sends an email but the customer gets nothing? How do I fix email notifications when an end user receives nothing?

If the trigger or notification rule and email appear on the ticket events but your End User or Customer or Client does not receive these emails, follow the email troubleshooting guide. Ensure that there wasn't a CC issue.

How do I safely test my notification rules? What is the best practice after I change auto-reply conditions? How do I verify trigger updates work?

Once you correct conditions or the order of your triggers or auto-replies, create a test ticket and ensure you are satisfied with the result.

articles.name: Troubleshooting common SLA issues
articles.title: Troubleshooting common SLA issues
articles.body:
If you're experiencing service level agreement (SLA) issues, use this guide to find solutions to the most common behaviors.

This article contains the topics below:

  • The badge shows Now after the target is breached
  • Breached targets do not take business hours into consideration
  • The new policy does not display
  • SLA applied only to some tickets
  • First reply time metric not working
  • SLA is not paused when ticket status is pending
  • Target hours showing incorrectly
  • Newly applied SLA executes additional breach event
  • Why don't I see an SLA badge when there's an SLA policy on the ticket?
  • Updates to schedules don’t reflect in SLA Targets

For general information about how SLAs function, see Viewing and understanding SLA targets.

The badge shows Now after the target is breached

The badge displays Now when the target changes after a breach. These changes include public comments, status changes, priority changes, or policy changes.

Breached targets do not take business hours into consideration

Even if you set up the target on business hours, the badge always shows the time in calendar hours. It doesn't matter if your target is in business or calendar hours.

The new policy does not display

Zendesk doesn't apply a newly created SLA policy to existing tickets, or Zendesk doesn't apply an updated SLA policy to tickets already using that SLA.

Explanation

SLAs are based on ticket events. For an SLA policy to match tickets, an event such as ticket creation or ticket update must happen on the ticket. Otherwise, the ticket shows no SLA information or continues to show the old SLA information.

In the example below, an SLA policy didn't apply because there wasn't an existing policy to apply the last time the ticket was updated, or the ticket didn't meet the policy's conditions.

In the example below, an SLA policy applies after a ticket update, after you create a policy or update the ticket to meet an existing policy's conditions:

SLA applied only to some tickets

An SLA doesn't apply to tickets that meet all the SLA policy conditions.

Explanation

This happens when tickets don't have a set ticket priority. Tickets must have a priority for the SLA target to apply. The ticket priority must be the system default field. SLAs don't apply using custom ticket fields.

Depending on the ticket priority, SLA policies can have different target times and types of hours of operation:

If the ticket doesn't have a priority to match the target time in the SLA policy, the target time doesn't apply and no timer displays until you add a priority.

This can also happen if you create the ticket with a private comment or internal note from a user who is not a light agent. For private tickets, first reply time SLA targets are deferred. The SLA target activates when the first public end-user comment is added, even if there are public agent comments before that point.

In the example below, Zendesk didn't apply an SLA policy because the ticket was created without a priority.

After you update the same ticket to have a priority, Zendesk applies the SLA policy.

First reply time metric not working

A newly created ticket doesn't show a timer for the first reply time metric even though all criteria for the SLA policy are met.

Explanation

This happens when an agent creates a ticket on behalf of an end user and the first comment is public. The first reply target is fulfilled at ticket creation because the agent submits the first public comment, which is the first reply.

In the example below, no first reply time metric applies because the agent created the ticket.

You can create exceptions in your advanced settings.

SLA is not paused when the ticket status is pending

The SLA timer doesn't pause when the ticket status changes to Pending.

Explanation

This happens because the ticket waits for agents to fulfill the first reply time or the next reply time metric. There are currently four SLA targets that don't pause in pending status: First Reply Time, Next Reply Time, Periodic Update Time, and Total Resolution Time. Reply metrics don't change based on ticket status. You fulfill reply metrics if an agent replies with a public comment to the requester.

The first reply time and the next reply time always use an end-user comment as the starting point and a public agent response as an endpoint. For more information, see Defining and using SLA policies.

Target hours showing incorrectly

Tickets with SLAs that have a target in business hours show a higher number of hours than the SLA metrics should have set.

Explanation

SLA badges always display in calendar hours. Real time remaining appears so agents can prioritize their work. Although you can choose to calculate the target time in business hours, the timer still displays time in calendar hours and won't take into consideration non-working days or holidays set in your account.

For a detailed explanation, see Why do I see differences in SLA target hours?

Newly applied SLA executes additional breach event

When Zendesk applies a new policy to a ticket that already had the SLA breached, even if the new policy is only a modified version of an existing policy, the system records another breach event at the time of the update.

Explanation

The system can't modify past ticket events history. When a new SLA application happens, the SLA counter always looks forward from that point.

Why don't I see an SLA badge when there's an SLA policy on the ticket?

There are several reasons why an SLA badge won't appear on a ticket with an SLA policy applied. For more information, see Why aren't SLA badges appearing?

Updates to schedules don’t reflect in SLA Targets

Explanation

When you edit a schedule, including adding or removing holidays, SLA targets don't automatically update. However, if a ticket receives an SLA update, targets reassess. If there's a public comment, status change, priority change, or policy change, the ticket recalculates its SLA badge according to the new schedule. Minimize schedule changes, since frequent changes cause confusion.

Note: SLAs don't apply to tickets that you solve upon creation. The solved status satisfies SLAs and prevents policies from activating, as long as the ticket remains solved. If you reopen tickets, SLAs can start activating and running normally.
THIS SECTION IS AI CONTENT. DON'T EDIT OR DELETE.

Why does the SLA badge or due date show "Now" after a breach? Why does the deadline change to "Now" after breaching?

The badge displays "Now" when the target changes after a breach. Public comments, ticket status or progress changes, priority changes, or policy changes cause these adjustments.

Why do breached SLA targets ignore business hours or schedules? Why do deadlines show calendar hours after breaching?

Even if you set up the target on business hours or schedules, the badge always shows the time in calendar hours. It does not matter if your target uses business or calendar hours.

Why does a new SLA policy or due date rule not display? Why does an updated policy not apply to existing tickets?

Zendesk does not apply a newly created SLA policy to existing tickets. Zendesk also does not apply an updated SLA policy to tickets already using that SLA. For an SLA policy to match tickets, an event such as ticket creation or a ticket update must happen on the ticket. Otherwise, the ticket shows no SLA information or continues to show the old SLA information. This happens when the ticket does not meet the policy conditions, or an existing policy does not exist to apply the last time you update the ticket.

Why does an SLA apply only to some tickets? Why do some deadlines or due dates fail to show on tickets?

An SLA does not apply to tickets that meet all the SLA policy conditions when tickets do not have a set ticket priority. Tickets must have a priority for the SLA target to apply. The ticket priority must use the system default field. SLAs do not apply using a custom field or extra dropdown. If the ticket does not have a priority to match the target time in the SLA policy, the target time does not apply and no timer displays until you add a priority.

This also happens if you create the ticket with a private comment or internal note from a user who is not a light agent or viewer. For private tickets, first reply time SLA targets defer. The SLA target activates when the first public comment from an end user or customer is added, even if public agent comments exist before that point.

Why is the first reply time metric or deadline not working? Why does a new ticket show no timer for the first reply time?

This happens when an agent or team member creates a ticket on behalf of an end user or customer and the first comment is public. The first reply target fulfills at ticket creation because the agent submits the first public comment, which acts as the first reply. You can create exceptions in your advanced settings.

Why is the SLA not paused when the ticket status is pending? Why does the deadline timer keep running in pending state or progress?

The SLA timer does not pause when the ticket status changes to Pending. This happens because the ticket waits for agents to fulfill the first reply time or the next reply time metric. Four SLA targets do not pause in pending status: First Reply Time, Next Reply Time, Periodic Update Time, and Total Resolution Time. Reply metrics do not change based on ticket status. You fulfill reply metrics if an agent replies with a public comment to the requester or customer. The first reply time and the next reply time always use an end-user comment as the starting point and a public agent response as an endpoint.

Why are SLA target hours showing incorrectly? Why do deadlines with business hours show a higher number of hours?

Tickets with SLAs that use a target in business hours or schedules show a higher number of hours than the SLA metrics should set. SLA badges always display in calendar hours. Real time remaining appears so agents can prioritize their work. Although you can choose to calculate the target time in business hours, the timer still displays time in calendar hours and does not take into consideration non-working days or holidays set in your account.

Why does a newly applied SLA execute an additional breach event? Why does updating a policy log another deadline breach?

When Zendesk applies a new policy to a ticket that already breached the SLA, even if the new policy is only a modified version of an existing policy, the system records another breach event at the time of the update. The system cannot modify past ticket events history. When a new SLA application happens, the SLA counter always looks forward from that point.

Why do updates to schedules, business hours, or holidays not reflect in SLA targets? Why does editing a schedule fail to update deadlines?

When you edit a schedule, including adding or removing holidays, SLA targets do not automatically update. However, if a ticket receives an SLA update, targets reassess. If a public comment, status change, priority change, or policy change occurs, the ticket recalculates its SLA badge according to the new schedule. Minimize schedule changes, since frequent changes cause confusion.

Do SLAs apply to tickets solved upon creation? Can a fixed in one go ticket trigger a deadline timer?

SLAs do not apply to tickets that you solve upon creation. The solved ticket status satisfies SLAs and prevents policies from activating, as long as the ticket remains solved. If you reopen tickets, SLAs can start activating and running normally.
